speaker: interviewer
text: --Los Angeles, Survivors of the Shoah Foundation. OK, before we stopped, we were talking about the trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were describing being in the wagon and being cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sleds--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sleds?

speaker: interviewee
text: --open sleds. And me just looking out and thinking, God, will the snow ever end? Just snow and snow and snow and more snow. And then at night, staying in peasants' huts, sleeping-- I remember one night-- on top of a-- they had a fireplace. And then they would have this shelf that came out over the fireplace. And you slept on that shelf. That's how you stayed warm. It could have been the-- it could have been something out of Tolstoy, out of the, you know, 17th, 18th century, 19th century. It was a-- it was a different world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember anything about-- what did you do all day? Or what, what did-- what was the conversation about? Do you remember what the adults were talking about or where you thought you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, I knew we were trying to get away. It wasn't clear where we were going. But I-- that I think, at that point, that still was not clear to anybody exactly where we were going to end up, but definitely to get away from the Germans, to get out of Poland. Maybe there was some mention of America, South America. That would have been still very, you know, rarely talked about, at least not that I recall it being talked about. What I remember is the physical experience. I remember one night when we were escaping, the whole family is in some kind of a barn. Somebody's obviously put us up for the night in this barn, part of this Underground Railroad thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here, there's about 12 of us, 14 of us in this barn, ranging from us, the little children, to my grandparents. The barn doors, like, fly open. And in come three Lithuanian soldiers, bayonets fixed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lithuanians had a reputation for being notoriously anti-Semitic. I mean, they're-- right up to the government level, which was also true in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again-- we talked about existential luck. [LAUGHS] One of these three soldiers turns out to be Jewish. Unheard of-- probably in the whole Lithuanian Army, there were 50 Jews. [LAUGHS] This guy was Jewish. He spoke Yiddish. And that's, of course, the marvel of Yiddish is that it was the one international language. If you were Jewish and you spoke Yiddish, you could go anywhere in the world. And you could converse with a person who otherwise would never have understood you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my uncles were able to negotiate with him, gave them vodka, gave him a watch, gave him some money. And these soldiers left and let us go. They could've shot us on the spot. They could have handed us back, as they often did, to the Germans. Were you on the Lithuanian side of the border now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were probably right on the border somewhere. And where they were catching Jews, they were sending them back to the Germans. That was the deal they had. But these guys let us go. And so we arrived in Wilno. I don't-- I can't tell you how we arrived there. Wilno is a real city. It, you know-- capital city of Lithuania. And we're going to be here for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we rent a-- some room somewhere. Now, after what I described about our life in Poland, this is mother, father, my sister, and I all in one tiny room. And that was the same for all my aunts and uncles.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the children, actually, it's a very, in some ways, magical time because we get a lot of time to play. We do go to school. We go to a school where they speak only Hebrew and Yiddish, neither one of which I know. I don't know how I get along in that school, but I do. And everybody is in a waiting mode. I think we're waiting for passports to get out of there, which is not something you get, you know, overnight. What we have going for us is that an uncle of mine-- one of my mother's brothers who had gotten out through Austria and had also been-- not only had his own money, but had married into one of the wealthier Jewish families in Austria who owned the largest Krupnik, which was the department store of Vienna, like the Macy's here. He gets out with money and, and goes to England and, eventually, South America. And he's trying to help us. So I think we're getting some money from him and some help, you know, financially and maybe trying to get us passports to get us into the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: But meanwhile, we're still stuck in-- Vilnius.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Lithuania, in Wilno. And so that's, that's the life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There for about eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Seems like a very long time. I mean, like you really settle. You have a life, a daily life, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember what school was like? You remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, all--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your first school--

speaker: interviewee
text: First school-- Hebrew, Yiddish. I have a photo-- I don't have that photograph. I wish I had it here. My sister has a photograph of me in my class with this teacher who I did terrific crush on [LAUGHS] plus all these other kids, sweet kids who, I think, were all killed later on by the Germans. There also in Lithuania, 95%, 98%, 99% of the Jews were, were murdered. Very, very few escaped with their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any-- describe what daily life was like. What were-- your mother was now cooking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother-- they were all cooking, all-- we-- it seems to me that my memory of my-- my memory of the life there is mostly with my cousins and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, playing games. There was a yard, a-- some kind of a courtyard or yard down there where we play with other children. What I do remember about that time for me is that the war has taken its toll on me in another way. One has been the loss of the governess, which I'm sure I'm in the process of going through then, you know. Even-- not that that was something I articulated. I don't ever remember my mother saying to me, how do you feel about that, or acknowledging, probably, even the loss. But what I do remember very clearly-- I mentioned before that the stories that scared me. More had happened. I remember one time-- I'm, I'm backtracking a little now. But I remember at one time-- and we're still in German-occupied Poland-- walking with my governess one evening on a cold night. I remember it being cold. I remember stars out. And I remember screams coming from a place where people are obviously being tortured. That's only something I put together later, but again, just with a child's sense that something is terribly, terribly wrong. My grandfather actually shaves off his beard. And I didn't even recognize him. Because he's told he's going to have to do that at that time if he thinks he can escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we are in Lithuania. And I'm feeling the effects of that with a physical fear. I was a very athletic child. I was riding a two-wheeled bicycle by the time I was five years old. I used to go skiing. I was skiing by the time I was four.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm afraid of things. I'm afraid to climb. I'm afraid to jump. I'm afraid of getting hurt. I'm sure I've been very hurt already. So that's what-- that's what's happening. And that's, that's the part of the war that I think often is not even talked about. Because it was such a life-and-death thing. And there was so much about people being murdered on the spot and dying in camps that the more subtle injuries and the more subtle wounds that then continue with you through your life are rarely, rarely talked about. I think there was a film I saw some years ago-- very good Dutch film. I think it was called or something like that that deals with a Dutch boy-- not Jewish, as a matter of fact-- whose family is murdered by the Nazis during the war because they think they're hiding, you know, underground people. And 30 or 40 years later on a vacation in Italy, he wakes up one morning in a cold sweat. It's that sort of thing that continues. So that's why I mentioned that because that's sort of a subtext to what's going on for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have any contact with non-Jewish Lithuanians? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: While we're there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --have any memories--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- no, I have no memory of that, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, again, is the world, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they-- how did they do the ritual? Or how did they-- did they try to do the holidays in that situation? Did they try and keep Shabbos? How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I don't remember. If we did it, I think it was done on the QT. And I think it's done, you know, it's not like-- obviously, like it was in Poland. So-- I do know my mother continues every night to say prayers with my sister and myself, you know. By that time, we-- I've learned about the evening prayer, which runs about three, four minutes. And she comes and sits by the bed. And I say it out loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight, nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what does this bring us to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Date-wise? We're somewhere now in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, who is the oldest of us-- I mean, already in her late teens-- actually, I think, is the one who goes and is able, finally, to get us those passports, I think, through Sugihara, that Japanese--

speaker: interviewer
text: Transit visas.

speaker: interviewee
text: --transit visas so that we will be able to get into Russia as a jump-off point of getting out of that part of the world. Because you, you can't get out of Europe anymore. People who got out earlier, who came, like, let's say-- you know, who went from Vienna to London by plane, all that is closed. All that has been now-- because of the war, World War II is raging. So the only way out will be through Russia. And so she gets us transit visas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember that? And you remember some discussion about OK, we're going to go. And where are we going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think now there is more talk about hopefully getting to-- through Russia, then Japan, Canada, and then hopefully, the United States. And if we can't get in the United States, we may end up having to go to South America, which is the two places people went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe leaving Vilnius.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't physically remember leaving Vilnius. I can't even tell you how we left. My guess is at that point-- by that point, we're traveling by train. This is no longer the traveling by sled, hiding from the enemy. Because so far, this is all still on the Russian control. Shortly thereafter, the war will break out. And the Germans will take over Lithuania. That will be a very different story for the Jews who are there. But we, actually, I think, leave by train. I remember getting to Moscow-- incredible experience. Incredible experience-- I mean, we stay at the Metropol Hotel, which is still there, I'm told. It's one of those hotels from czarist Russian days. I've never seen a place that big. We keep, keep getting lost-- the kids. We played there to see if we can find certain stairs. Like, there's one stairwell where there is the big stuffed bear on its hind paws. We go looking for that, you know. The hotel seems so big to us that we look for, for hours before we can find that particular staircase. My sister-- this I love-- my sister reads us-- me and-- my cousins and myself sit around. And she will read us in Polish in Moscow. [LAUGHS] They-- that's, that's sort of also the story of the war, the dislocation, the movement, you know, the mixing of things. So here we're being read a British author, 19th-century author, in a Russian capital in Polish. And-- but I remember taking walks with my father in Red Square. I remember, you know, the child's sense of it being huge and vast with those cupolas, you know, for the churches and-- I mean, those-- you know, the Byzantine, Greek-Orthodox style of architecture they have there. We're there for a bunch of weeks. We're there for some weeks, again, probably waiting for transit papers or train things to come through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do, do you think that, from your point of view, or that you're just-- that there is an underlying fear and dread at this point? Or is it just an adventure or-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's becoming less dreadful because we are out-- I think there's a sense that we're out of Hitler's grasp. And you must understand this is before anybody knows anything about concentration camps or, you know, or mass-- but everybody knows-- the Jews have known for a long time that Hitler is very bad news. The Russians are not pursuing at that point any specific anti-Jewish program. So we're already in a much safer place. Plus we're not planning to stay there. So-- and now we're traveling by train. Because the next part of the journey now takes us from Moscow to Vladivostok-- I think, 10 or 11 days by train, an incredible distance. I mean, somebody showed that to me on a map. I think it is the equivalent-- I'm not far off-- of taking a train from New York to Los Angeles back to New York and halfway to Los Angeles again. That's how far it is. It's like 10,000-- I mean, enormous distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're on this train. And again, snow, snow, snow. I'm thinking, is the snow-- Do you have clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: --ever going to end? Yes, well, we're in a train. You know, it's not cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're not-- this is not I mean, snow isn't blowing in through the chinks. This is a train. Stories are being told. You know, we do little drawings. We keep it-- you know, children get restless. We run up and down the aisles. We do whatever children do. But we are-- the sense is we are going toward something hopefully better and away from something that was very bad. And so we-- this train takes us to Vladivostok. We then take a boat to Japan. Japan, I mean, [LAUGHS] talk about exotic. [LAUGHS] That is really an incredible experience. It's like the-- I know that we-- I don't know the sequence on this. I know that we get to-- at some point, we're in Yokohama briefly. And I think we sail out of Kobe, which is another port town when we finally move on from Japan. But we stay in Tokyo for a couple of weeks. Somehow, there must be that Underground Railroad, you know, with names being passed along. My family finds probably the one kosher restaurant in Tokyo-- a kosher restaurant in Tokyo in 1940. I mean, it boggles the mind, you know. But I think they find it. I remember Japan being extremely clean. And people-- the family marvel at that. I remember them talking about it. The street-- I mean, not another speck of paper, nothing. You know, people wearing masks if they have a cold, so they won't spread germs-- very different, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And already some kind of early-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how can I put it? Sort of, like, hints of America because Japan is influenced by America the way Poland was not before the war. So that, for instance, it's the first time I see those barbershops with the rotating poles which no longer exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's what [LAUGHS] they used to have. And, and in fact, I go to get a haircut. And they let you sit, like, on a hobbyhorse while they cut your hair-- sort of American ideas, but still very, very Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now there's talk-- more talk. Are we going to be able to get into America, this promised land that I'm hearing stories about? Or is it going to-- are we going to end up having to go to South America? And so how-- when you left Japan, you-- obviously, by this time, whatever mechanism they had for getting papers, you had papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have papers to take us as far as Canada. Not America yet-- it's still not clear. We go to America-- I think, when we're on the boat going from Japan to Canada-- that we hear the election results that Roosevelt has been re-elected. Roosevelt is going to be the great, white father, whatever it is, you know, who, who maybe can help us, or something. That's-- that-- I hear the name mentioned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have some distant relatives in Canada. We get to Canada. We don't have the clothes for it. I remember my mother goes to a-- something probably like ORT, you know, one of those Jewish committees set up for refugees, and gets me some-- what I'm wearing are short pants with stockings-- wool stockings, which is very humiliating for a boy, you know. I mean, that's not the way the kids are dressing in school in Canada. Because I'm put in a school there when we get there. And so she gets me the pants that boys wear in Canada, which were like jodhpurs at that time. And but we first, first-- that's when we went to Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we get to Vancouver. We stay there for the first-- for about a month or two. First time I've ever seen an English movie-- a movie in English. I don't understand a word of it. It's like a dream. I walk in there. And I watched this movie. And you have to try to figure out what it is, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt-- one of my aunts is from England, Jewish but from England. And so on the boat over, she has taught us 10 words, [LAUGHS] you know what I'm saying, that kind of thing. And we're put immediately into schools. You know, there are no classes for refugee kids. So I don't speak any English, of course. One of my memories which I have of that experience of coming to England-- I mean, sorry, coming to Canada is that the first day or two I think until we're put up somewhere else, we're staying in this little hotel. And they bring us breakfast. And they bring out cereal. They bring out probably Kellogg's Flakes or something like that. And I, who had been raised with a chef-- I mean, with-- excuse me, with a guy-- with a cook who cooked like a chef, see these cornflakes in my plate. And I am tremendously impressed because I think each one of these has been made by hand. And I think, God, this is fancy. After all that running and living, you know, [LAUGHS] and we're eating these little handmade [LAUGHS] flakes. That was my great first sort of introduction to Western eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you actually feel like you were going to stay there? Or did you sense--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that where-- you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still-- it was still between-- now between the United States and South America. We're not going to stay in Canada. I think we can, probably. I don't know. But we're not going to stay there. There's a good-- I think it's looking more and more likely now that we will get into the United States. But we either have to wait for our visa number to come up or something to happen. I don't know what that something is.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go to school there. So you're starting to learn English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting to learn English, wondering how all those kids speak this language. I think that all the kids are geniuses because they all speak so quickly-- [LAUGHS] they speak this language. And we sing 'God Save the King' in school every morning and listen to the radio. Yeah, but that's sort of-- that's the doorway to America. And finally, whatever the green light is-- you know, whether our number comes up, whether the visas are finally gotten, we go to the United States. And we go to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you get to New York, you assume this is-- this is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is it. And so then begins the new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: The new life still obviously flowing out of the old life, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your parents live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are put up-- my uncle is the one with the money. The one who got out of Austria lives in New York-- a very elegant apartment on the West Side. We are initially put in little, rented apartments in a hotel on the West Side called the Hotel Churchill. New York, at that point-- this is 1941, but before the United States has gotten into the war. New York-- Upper West Side of New York feels to me, as a child, like the way the movie feels when you see it today, full of refugees-- German, Polish, whatever-- sitting in cafeterias, smoking endless cigarettes, discussing you know, their situation. It's very war-influenced, that part of the city, because there's so many refugees who are at least temporarily staying there. And we're staying in this little apartment, two rooms-- one for my parents, one for my sister and me. But cramped and tiny as that little place is compared to what I have known as a young child, there is definitely this feeling. And I remember, I think, asking my parents that repeatedly.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not going to have to move anymore? Because up to that point, it's always a feeling that I move, you move, you move, you move, you move, you-- there is no home. So if they had given us one room, it would have felt great. And did they try to construct their lives as Jews in New York? How do they do that? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They joined a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came observant in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were always observant. That had never stopped. I mean, the very fact that we found a kosher restaurant [LAUGHS] in Tokyo speaks to the desire to be observant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents joined a synagogue near where we live. I still remember its name, the West Side Institutional Synagogue, about a few blocks from where we live. We go every Saturday morning for the services. We are enrolled in their Sunday School-- my sister and I, you know, and cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father has a job. My father's life is pretty unhappy once we come to the States. He had married-- by marrying my mother, he had married into a wealthy family. In Poland, he had become-- by marrying my mother, he had been named one of the directors of this very large, prosperous family. And we lived that way. By the time we come to New York, my grandfather-- who was the nominal, at least, head of, you know, titular head of the family-- he dies of cancer shortly after we come to the, you know, the United States, say, within six or eight months of the time we come there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father's future, you might say, is at the discretion financially of his brothers-in-law, who are not that favorably disposed to him. And while they become-- my, my two uncles who have traveled out with us join the uncle who had come through Cuba and the one with the money in becoming directors of this new chocolate family-- factory which they started in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father becomes the expediter there. You know, he runs basically-- a sort of a glorified shipping clerk. And I remember that as a child. I remember that being-- feeling that myself that there was a definite status change which he-- you know, which affects him very hard, affects me. I guess it probably affects all of us. And shortly after we come to the United States, within a year of the time we come to the United States, my father has his first heart attack, also very connected with the war. I mean, connected with the fact that he smokes a lot and does that. He's a young man. He's only in his 40s. His family-- all his brothers and sisters except for two have been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he doesn't know that yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knows at that point that they are not getting out. He doesn't know they're dead yet. But he knows that they're in a lot of trouble. He was very, very close to his family. He's left them behind. And his future here is diminished anyway. I think his heart is broken basically. And I think that contributes certainly, you know, to what happens to him in the years that follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, you're in school. Your sister is in school. And you're assimilating--

speaker: interviewee
text: Assimilating-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --as young Americans in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: PS 87, near where we live, yes, learning English very quickly. Again, no classes at that point for kids who don't speak. You just-- they put you in, and you learn. And, of course, at my age, you learn very quickly. So that quickly, you know, disappears for me as a problem. It seems to me that after about a year here, that's not a problem at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- you have any memories of people talking about the war? I mean, when the United States enters the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or the memory of the bombing of Pearl Harbor? But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what, what, what do-- I mean, what are your memories for what people were saying about what was happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The news in Europe is all bad. The Germans have begun, you know, to take over. Somewhere in there, they've attacked Russia and are sweeping across Russia. Everything is gone in Europe. I mean, France has gone down. England is the only thing left. There is a hope that somehow America will become involved in the war. But the general feeling is that they probably won't because the sentiment in America is, you know, is not to join the war for most people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when, when Pearl Harbor comes, I remember there'd be-- and America gets involved in the war, there's a feeling, oh, good, you know. Now maybe something will happen-- that sort of a feeling. Yeah, I remember that. Do you remember having any, any memories or any discussions about the, the Jews who were there already versus these immigrant Jews and some kind of separation or, I don't know, some tensions between--

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not really. I didn't feel that. There, there was such a large influx of refugee Jews into the West Side at that time. I don't remember that being the case. I remember a couple of times once or twice in school as a kid having some kind of a minor-- and maybe not so minor traumatic experiences, like, with a teacher one day saying to me-- because I-- we had gone on an outing, and I think I dropped a piece of paper on the ground. And sort of bawling me out in front of the class and saying, well, if you don't like it here, you know, you should go back where you came from, something brilliant like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was-- no, you know, we're in a very-- we're not in Oklahoma, you know what I mean? We're in-- we're in the West Side of New York, which is Jewish already. And I think most of the people who come over get assimilated pretty quickly into the life of-- in that respect, of the city. But it still has its own identity, you know what I mean? These people go to Schrafft's, which is like a place where you go for coffee. There's like a world within a world that, that exists for the Jews, yes. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] your-- you, you go to school. And then how, how does your family talk about it? What, what, what are their--

speaker: interviewee
text: The war?

speaker: interviewer
text: The war and sort of what their expectations are for you and what Poland or what life was like before, what you're supposed to become now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm, mhm--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah. There's a huge adjustment going on, probably harder in some way for my parents on a physical level than it is for my sister and me. They do not speak English. My father starts going to night school, gets a diploma that he's very proud of, reads My mother's English comes slower because she's not so much out in the world. She's furiously calling up friends and relatives, asking them how to prepare meals. Because she's never cooked before, really. I don't know how we did it earlier. But now she has to learn how to be a housewife. She does it very well. And she does it. It's not-- she doesn't, you know, not do it. But it's not easy. It's, it's pretty hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still speak Polish in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still speak Polish in the house, yes. And my sister and I, of course, less and less so. Certainly, out of the house, we're speaking English. But at home, my mother-- partially because her English is poor and partially because she doesn't want us to lose the second language-- always says, speak Polish, speak Polish. However, the feeling about Poland and what's going on, that's a whole area unto itself. Because my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father is trying to get news constantly about his sister and his brothers. And he's-- you know, I remember him writing letters and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] waiting to hear the news. And the news is never good.
